ex:tesla-us685957-1901.text: The granted patent claims an apparatus that charges a capacitor from 'radiant energy' — Tesla's term for sunlight and other ray sources striking an elevated insulated plate — and discharges it through a controller. The claims cover electrostatic collection from natural sources, not extraction of vacuum energy.

ex:bearden-meg-2002.text: United States patent 6,362,718 (2002) covers the device's construction: a permanent magnet, a nanocrystalline flux-path core and switched input coils. A granted patent certifies novelty of the claimed construction; it involves no test of over-unity performance.

ex:howard-johnson-1979.text: United States patent 4,151,431 ('Permanent magnet motor', 1979) was granted for the magnet-gate geometry. Patent examination covers novelty and description, not energy balance; no independent measurement of net work output is on record.

ex:correa-pagd.text: United States patent 5,416,391 (1995) describes the pulsed abnormal glow discharge regime the reactors used: a discharge region where current falls as voltage rises. The negative-resistance regime is established discharge physics; the energy-gain claim rests on the inventors' own calorimetry.

ex:garrett-1935.text: United States patent 2,006,676 (1935) shows the construction: an electrolytic cell generating hydrogen and oxygen fed to the engine's intake. The arrangement moves energy from the battery through electrolysis to combustion; the patent documents the plumbing, not a net gain.

ex:cook-battery-1871.text: United States patent 119,825 ('Improvement in induction-coils', 1871) is the primary artifact of this record. The specification describes two coils feeding each other; no working self-running unit was ever examined on record.

ex:shoulders-ev-clusters.text: United States patent 5,018,180 ('Energy conversion using high charge density', 1991) is the central document of the bench corpus: it specifies cathode geometry, pulse conditions and witness-plate observations for the micron-scale charge clusters Shoulders called EVs.

ex:gray-ema-motor.text: United States patent 4,595,975 ('Efficient power supply suitable for inductive loads', 1986) documents the capacitor-discharge circuit behind the motor demonstrations. The patent certifies the circuit topology; the 'cold electricity' interpretation and the efficiency claims were never independently measured.

ex:wallace-kinemassic-1971.text: United States patent 3,626,605 ('Method and apparatus for generating a secondary gravitational force field', 1971) is one of the few granted patents claiming a laboratory gravitational effect, from spin-aligned rotating masses. No independent replication of the claimed field is on record.

ex:puharich-water-1983.text: United States patent 4,394,230 ('Method and apparatus for splitting water molecules', 1983) documents the resonant electrolysis circuit. The patent's own theory section asserts efficiencies beyond thermodynamic limits; the grant certifies the circuit description, not the energy claim.

ex:meyer-wfc-1996.text: United States patent 4,936,961 ('Method for the production of a fuel gas', 1990) is the core water-fuel-cell patent: a capacitive cell driven by voltage pulses through a resonant charging choke. The claimed sub-Faradaic gas yield was not sustained in the court-ordered examination the base record cites.

ex:hooper-motional-field.text: United States patent 3,610,971 ('All-electric motional electric field generator', 1971) documents the equal-and-opposite-current coil arrangement. Hooper's claim that the motional field of conduction electrons penetrates shielding unlike electrostatic fields is stated in the patent itself; the record's standing rests on the few reported bench tests.

ex:podkletnov-1992.text: Giovanni Modanese's theoretical analysis (arXiv:hep-th/9505094) is the main published attempt to model the reported shielding within quantum gravity; it concluded the effect would require anomalously strong gravitational vacuum polarization. It analyzes the claim, it does not confirm the measurement.

ex:pioneer-resolution-2012.text: Turyshev and collaborators' thermal-recoil analysis (arXiv:1204.2507, published in Physical Review Letters) accounted for the full anomalous acceleration using anisotropic heat radiation from the spacecraft's power and instrument compartments, with no new physics required.

ex:opera-resolution-2012.text: The collaboration's revised measurement report (arXiv:1109.4897, final version) documents both instrumental faults — a loose optical-fiber connector and a clock oscillator out of tolerance — and the corrected flight time, consistent with light speed.

ex:mpemba-1969.text: Burridge and Linden (Scientific Reports 6:37665, 2016) reviewed the measurement history and could not reproduce a robust effect once thermometer placement was controlled; they argue much of the literature measured cooling-curve artifacts. Whether the effect exists under controlled protocols remains contested.

ex:maddox-check-1988.text: The investigation report ('High-dilution experiments a delusion', Nature 334:287, 1988) documents the on-site protocol: under blinded sample coding the laboratory's effect disappeared. It is the primary document of this examination.

ex:polywater-resolution.text: Derjaguin and Churaev's closing paper (Nature 244:430, 1973) reports that rigorously cleaned samples showed no anomalous properties, ending the episode from within the group that had championed it.

ex:hayasaka-takeuchi-1989.text: The original report is Physical Review Letters 63:2701 (1989): right-rotating gyroscopes appeared to lose weight in proportion to rotor speed. Independent replications at higher sensitivity, one of them linked from this timeline, found no effect.

ex:faller-null-1990.text: Faller and collaborators' null result (Physical Review Letters 64:825, 1990) bounded any rotation-dependent weight change well below the claimed signal, using a balance sensitive enough to have seen it.

ex:saxl-allen-1970.text: The measurement was published in Physical Review D 3:823 (1971): a charged torsion pendulum's period lengthened during the March 1970 solar eclipse. The paper is both the record's strength and its limit — one instrument, one event, no independent repetition.

ex:emdrive-eagleworks-2016.text: The Journal of Propulsion and Power paper (2017) reports 1.2 mN/kW in vacuum and names thermal drift as the leading error candidate. The Dresden replications this timeline links later reproduced signals of this size from thermal and magnetic artifacts.

ex:google-lenr-2019.text: The program summary (Nature 569:45, 2019) reports no excess heat under the tested conditions across three approaches, while arguing that the unexplored parameter space justifies continued measurement. It is a rare example of an adversarially funded re-examination with fully published methods.

ex:rossi-ecat-2011.text: The closest thing to an independent test on record is the 2013 Levi et al. report (arXiv:1305.3913), whose authors were granted supervised but not full control of the device; its power-balance methodology drew detailed published criticism. No fully independent replication exists.

ex:michelson-morley-1887.text: The original paper (American Journal of Science s3-34:333, 1887) reports a fringe-shift bound roughly forty times smaller than the expected aether-wind signal. It is the reference point every later drift claim in this lineage is measured against.

ex:shankland-1955.text: The reanalysis (Reviews of Modern Physics 27:167, 1955) traced Miller's periodic signals to temperature gradients across the interferometer hut, using Miller's own data sheets. It is the canonical worked example of an anomaly dissolving under systematic-error analysis.

ex:tajmar-esa-2006.text: The original report is arXiv:gr-qc/0603033: accelerometer signals near spinning superconducting rings, framed as a gravitomagnetic moment far above general-relativistic expectation. The group's own later measurements with improved isolation did not sustain the effect.

ex:wood-nrays-1904.text: Wood's one-page account (Nature 70:530, 1904) describes removing the essential prism unnoticed while the laboratory continued to report N-ray readings. It remains the standard reference for observer-expectation effects in instrument reading.

ex:mills-hydrino.text: Rathke's analysis (New Journal of Physics 7:127, 2005) is the main peer-reviewed examination of the theory: it argues the claimed below-ground hydrogen states rest on a mathematical error in the modified quantum mechanics. Replies and counter-replies exist; no independent spectroscopic confirmation is on record.

ex:taleyarkhan-2002.text: The original claim is Science 295:1868 (2002): neutron and tritium signatures coincident with acoustic cavitation in deuterated acetone. The paper is where the evidential chain starts; the contested replication record that followed is what settled the claim's standing.

ex:spawar-cr39.text: The triple-track report (Naturwissenschaften 96:135, 2009) is the strongest single artifact: CR-39 detectors showing patterns the authors attribute to energetic neutrons. The dispute the base record notes centers on whether chemical and mechanical damage can mimic the tracks.

ex:montagnier-2011.text: The claim's primary document is arXiv:1012.5166: electromagnetic signals from filtered, highly diluted DNA solutions, plus a claimed remote transfer of sequence information. No independent laboratory has reproduced the protocol under blinded conditions.

ex:cen-spectrum-2014.text: The accidental spectrum (Physical Review Letters 112:035001, 2014) is the record's substance: field spectrographs caught a natural ball-lightning event whose emission lines matched soil elements, supporting a vaporized-silicon pathway for at least this instance.

ex:fischbach-fifth-force-1986.text: The reanalysis (Physical Review Letters 56:3, 1986) reported a composition-dependent pattern in Eötvös's torsion data and proposed a fifth force at laboratory range. A decade of dedicated experiments, closed by the torsion-balance program this timeline links, found no such force.

ex:nasa-lattice-2020.text: The published measurement (Physical Review C 101:044609, 2020) reports deuteron-deuteron fusion products in bremsstrahlung-irradiated deuterated metals, framed as electron-screened lattice confinement. It is conventional nuclear physics under unusual conditions, peer-reviewed and replicable by method — not an over-unity claim.

ex:jones-neutrons-1989.text: The Nature paper (338:737, 1989) claimed a neutron rate barely above background — physically distinct from the excess-heat announcement it is often conflated with. Later work by the same community treated the result as unconfirmed.

ex:fleischmann-pons-1989.text: The primary document is the Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry paper (261:301, 1989), submitted in haste and corrected in errata; its calorimetric claim, not the press conference, is what the replication record actually tested.

ex:caltech-mit-null-1989.text: Lewis and collaborators' recheck (Nature 340:525, 1989) is one of the key null documents: calorimetry and nuclear-product searches on palladium-deuterium cells found nothing above background at the claimed levels.

ex:iwamura-2002.text: The permeation-transmutation report (Japanese Journal of Applied Physics 41:4642, 2002) documents element-specific surface signatures after deuterium permeation through layered palladium. Partial third-party repetitions have reported both supporting and null outcomes.

doc:bearden-scalar-em.text: For the scalar-wave question the doctrine turns on, the peer-reviewed treatment is Reed and Hively (Symmetry 12:2110, 2020): a gauge-free extended electrodynamics in which scalar and longitudinal modes exist with stated, falsifiable properties. It gives the discourse a testable formulation, which the doctrine's own texts do not.

ex:meyl-scalar-kits.text: Whether Maxwell's framework leaves room for scalar or longitudinal radiation depends on which potential-level degrees of freedom survive gauge fixing. That question — what the standard formulation removed and what would change if it had not — is analyzed in 'The Deleted Degrees of Freedom' (Advanced Rediscovery, 2026).

ex:sweet-vta-1980s.text: The device's theoretical framing came from the scalar-electromagnetics school: Bearden co-authored the VTA's 'phase conjugate' account and made the device a centerpiece of that doctrine. The doctrine record collects that framework and its evidential standing.

ex:hutchison-corpus.text: The material-damage subset of this corpus — bored channels, locally melted metal without bulk heating — has its closest documented relative in the charge-cluster bench corpus, where similar witness marks were produced repeatably at small scale and tied to a conventional cathode-spot mechanism under discussion.

ex:kapanadze-videos.text: The builder community that circulated these videos commonly describes the device as derived from Donald L. Smith's resonance energy designs; the Smith record collects that lineage's origin claims and their evidential standing.

ex:pais-navy-patents.text: United States patent 10,144,532 B2 ('Craft using an inertial mass reduction device', granted 2018, assigned to the US Navy) is the central document; it has since lapsed. The grant certifies the filing, not the physics — the Navy's own 2016-2019 test programme, released under FOIA, concluded the claimed effect could not be demonstrated.

ex:moddel-casimir-2021.text: The peer-reviewed core is Physical Review Research 3, L022007 (2021): conductance of metal-insulator-metal devices changes when one side carries a Casimir cavity, which the authors attribute to suppressed vacuum optical modes. The interpretation, not the measurement, is where the dispute lives — the second law is the stake.

ex:silvertooth-1986.text: The Nature correspondence (322:590, 1986) is the claim's formal anchor: a standing-wave interferometer reading interpreted as ~378 km/s absolute motion toward Leo — numerically close to the CMB dipole measured later. Critics locate the signal in the detection scheme rather than the aether; no accepted replication followed.

ex:alzofon-1994.text: The primary document is AIAA-81-1608 (17th Joint Propulsion Conference, 1981), a 34-page Boeing-affiliated design paper proposing gravity control by microwave-driven dynamic nuclear orientation. It is a formally presented proposal; the sole claimed bench test survives only in posthumous secondary accounts.

ex:barbosa-leal-captor-2013.text: International application WO 2013/104041 A1 ('Electromagnetic device for capturing free space electrons to generate electricity') documents the claimed coil-and-ground arrangement. An application publishes the design; it certifies neither grant nor performance, and documented reconstruction attempts did not reproduce the claims.

ex:stclair-applications-2000s.text: The best-known document of the cluster is published application US 2006/0145019 A1 ('Triangular spacecraft'). All entries in the corpus are applications — published design documents that were never granted and never accompanied by measurements.

ex:lk99-2023.text: The claim's primary document is arXiv:2307.12008 (22 July 2023). Its evidential weight sat in a resistivity drop, magnetic anomalies and a partial-levitation video; every one of those observables was later reproduced by independent groups and attributed to a copper-sulfide impurity transition rather than superconductivity.

ex:lk99-replications-2023.text: The consolidated replication study (arXiv:2311.03558) reproduced the reported anomalies and located them in the first-order structural transition of the Cu2S impurity near 385 K. The episode ran claim-to-resolution in roughly three weeks — a useful calibration point for how fast adversarial examination can work when a claim is cheap to synthesize.

ex:dias-csh-2020.text: The retraction note (Nature, 26 September 2022) is the decisive document: it records that the magnetic-susceptibility plots relied on a non-standard, user-defined background subtraction whose details the paper did not specify, and that its validity was called into question — while the authors maintained the raw data support the claims.

ex:dias-luh-2023.text: The retraction note (Nature, 7 November 2023) records the co-authors' own stated view that the paper does not accurately reflect the provenance of the investigated materials, the experimental measurements undertaken and the data-processing protocols applied. A paper unmade by its own author list is rare; the note is the primary document of that event.
